explanation:
Applied physics solves real-world problems: from free fall to household electricity. Formulas validated by centuries of experimentation.

## How It's Calculated

This calculator applies the formula using your input values, checking typical ranges and alerting when values fall outside reasonable parameters.

## Final Notes

This calculation is for reference only. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult a specialized professional. Data is current to 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: Why does water pressure increase about 1 atmosphere every 10 meters?
faq.a: Because water has a density of 1000 kg/m³. Every 10m of depth adds approximately 1 atmosphere of hydrostatic pressure.
Total pressure underwater = atmospheric pressure + hydrostatic pressure. At 10m depth in ocean water: 1 atm (atmospheric) + 1 atm (hydrostatic) = 2 atm total.
Military submarines: typically 500–700m (~70 atm). Research submersibles: the Challenger Deep reaches ~10.9 km (~1,100 atm).

faq.q: Does surface area affect hydrostatic pressure?
faq.a: No. Hydrostatic pressure depends only on depth and fluid density, not on container surface area. This principle is Pascal's law.

faq.q: What's the formula for water pressure at depth?
faq.a: P = ρ × g × h, where ρ is fluid density (kg/m³), g is gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²), and h is depth (m).

faq.q: How do pressure gauges use mercury?
faq.a: Mercury (ρ = 13,550 kg/m³) is used in manometers because its high density creates pressure changes proportional to small height differences.
